# enot - full reference

An earthy color scheme for vim, lightline, Sublime Text, VS Code, wezterm, mc, ranger, Nimble Commander and Telegram Desktop: dark and light themes, gruvbox character, built in CIELAB, with a machine-checked guarantee of distinguishability under color vision deficiency. Every value is generated by code; not a single color was picked by hand.

The name is the Russian word for raccoon. It reads the same in most languages, spells "tone" backwards, and the animal itself - grey fur with a high-contrast mask - is the scheme's formula: neutral backgrounds, distinguishable accents.

## Why

- about 8% of men and 0.5% of women have congenital color vision deficiency; nearly all of it is red-green: protanopia, deuteranopia and their anomalous degrees;
- earthy themes encode token roles almost purely by hue: accent lightness and chroma are equalized for a calm look; dichromacy collapses the chromatic plane onto a single axis, reducing such coding to one ochre band - the iso-lightness prototype of this scheme had a minimum pairwise CIEDE2000 of 0.7 between accents under deuteranopia, with the noticeability threshold near 1;
- CVD-aware themes exist (modus themes, GitHub Primer, vim-dichromatic), but all of them are cold or neutral; gruvbox and everforest offer neither CVD variants nor contrast guarantees.

The goal: keep the warm aesthetics and provide a measurable guarantee - every color pair stays distinguishable with normal vision, protanopia and deuteranopia.

## How it is built

- the space is CIELAB (LCh coordinates, D65 white point); backgrounds and text sit on fixed lightness steps, accents are optimization variables;
- dichromacy is simulated in linear sRGB: protanopia and deuteranopia per Viénot 1999, tritanopia per Brettel 1997; matrices from libDaltonLens;
- pair distinguishability is CIEDE2000 after simulation; the pair metric is the minimum over three visions: normal, protan, deutan;
- the optimizer maximizes the minimum pairwise distinguishability: greedy farthest-point initialization, coordinate ascent on an LCh grid, multi-start from three seeds;
- constraints: hue sectors that keep gruvbox roles recognizable (red 20-45, olive 100-135, teal 210-255 and so on), a lightness corridor, the sRGB gamut, contrast to background at least 4.5:1, at least ΔE00 10 away from the text and comment colors, minimum red chroma C* 22 - red is the diagnostics color;
- after maximization an aesthetic pass nudges each role toward its gruvbox anchor without lowering the achieved minimum (zero slack), so the full distinguishability spread is preserved;
- ANSI 16: four neutral slots spread along the lightness axis; six color families live in two disjoint lightness tiers - the family is recognized by its tier, and dichromacy does not distort lightness; in the light theme bright colors are darker than normal ones, as in gruvbox light;
- the spec: resolve.py builds colors.json - every theme role at three depths: hex (true color), xterm-256 index, ANSI slot; the 256 depth is not naive quantization (the xterm grid is so sparse in the muted region that nearest colors collapse roles down to ΔE00 = 0) - the same max-min optimizer runs over grid candidates anchored to the true-color values;
- the port engine (build.py) renders every port under ports/ from colors.json; a port substitutes ready values and contains no color math;
- regression: check.py verifies the colors.json invariants at every depth and that every color literal in the generated themes belongs to the spec; make check fails the build on any violation.

The key lever is lightness: an iso-lightness palette is impossible in principle - for seven accents on one LCh circle the ceiling of min ΔE00 is about 5. Opening the accent lightness window to the full range the background contrast allows (about 59-92 on dark, 26-46 on light) and letting chroma reach the gamut edge lifts the floor far past a narrow corridor. The shipped values: 13.3 for accents on dark and 11.1 on light, 7.2 for ANSI 16. At the 256 depth the accent minima are 8.3 (dark) and 3.8 (light): the light theme hits the physical ceiling of the xterm grid at 4.5:1 contrast, the full guarantee is carried by the true-color variants. Tritanopia (around 0.01% of the population) is not a hard constraint; it is simulated only for the site's live vision switcher.

## Research basis

- difference metric: Sharma, Wu, Dalal. The CIEDE2000 color-difference formula: implementation notes. Color Research and Application 30(1), 2005;
- dichromacy simulation: Brettel, Viénot, Mollon. Computerized simulation of color appearance for dichromats. JOSA A 14(10), 1997; Viénot, Brettel, Mollon. Digital video colourmaps for checking the legibility of displays by dichromats. Color Research and Application 24(4), 1999;
- model selection: Burrus. Review of open source color blindness simulations. daltonlens.org, 2021 - Viénot for protan and deutan, Brettel for tritan; Machado 2009 is valid for anomalous trichromacy but not for full dichromacy;
- CVD-safe categorical palettes: Okabe, Ito. Color universal design. 2002; Wong. Points of view: color blindness. Nature Methods 8, 441, 2011; Tol. Colour schemes. SRON technical note SRON/EPS/TN/09-002; Petroff. Accessible color sequences for data visualization. arXiv:2107.02270, 2021 - its "max-min perceptual distance over simulated visions" formulation matches the one used here;
- capacity of categorical color coding: Healey. Choosing effective colours for data visualization. IEEE Visualization, 1996 - about 7 categories are preattentively reliable; Boynton. Eleven colors that are almost never confused. 1989;
- contrast and polarity: WCAG 2.2, criteria 1.4.3 and 1.4.6; APCA (WCAG 3 candidate) - required contrast depends on size, weight and polarity; Piepenbrock, Mayr, Buchner. Positive display polarity is particularly advantageous for small character sizes. Human Factors 56(5), 2014 - the rationale for a first-class light theme;
- physiology of blue: the foveal center has almost no S-cones, and longitudinal chromatic aberration defocuses short wavelengths - pure blue is unfit for small text, so teal (hue 216) plays the role of blue.

## Tooling

- palette.py - shared constants, LCh to sRGB math and the theme assembly from enot.json;
- optimize.py - the accents and ANSI 16 optimizer (enot.json);
- resolve.py - the colors.json spec (three color depths);
- build.py - the port engine: renders every port under ports/ into dist/ from the spec;
- assets.py - the README design reference SVGs;
- cvd.py - the dichromacy simulation matrices and CIEDE2000;
- check.py - the single regression over invariants and artifacts;
- sitedata.py - the site data bundle: precomputed simulations, metrics, gates, the port registry;
- the modules live in pipeline/; make with no arguments prints the targets, make build runs the full cycle.

## Installation

vim and neovim: copy dist/vim/colors/enot.vim to ~/.vim/colors/ or ~/.config/nvim/colors/, then:

    set termguicolors
    set background=dark   " or light
    colorscheme enot

lightline: copy dist/lightline/autoload/lightline/colorscheme/enot.vim to ~/.vim/autoload/lightline/colorscheme/ (or the same path under your nvim config) and set let g:lightline = {'colorscheme': 'enot'}. One file serves both themes by reading &background when lightline loads; mode chips sit on accent fills with bg0 text, so the statusline carries the same contrast guarantee as the editor.

Sublime Text: copy dist/sublime/*.sublime-color-scheme into Packages/User (Preferences > Browse Packages) and pick the scheme via UI: Select Color Scheme; setting color_scheme to auto with dark_color_scheme and light_color_scheme follows the OS appearance.

VS Code: code --install-extension dist/vscode/enot.vsix (or Extensions > Install from VSIX), then pick enot-dark or enot-light in Preferences: Color Theme; window.autoDetectColorScheme with the two themes as preferences follows the OS. The extension carries workbench colors, token rules and the optimized terminal ANSI 16.

Nimble Commander: import dist/nimble/enot-dark.json and enot-light.json in Settings > Themes, then set them as the dark and light entries of automatic switching. The themes cover panels, tabs, the built-in terminal (ANSI 16 from the spec) and the viewer; file coloring matches mc and ranger: directories blue, executables green, links aqua, marked files yellow.

wezterm: copy dist/wezterm/*.toml to ~/.config/wezterm/colors/ and set color_scheme = "enot-dark" or "enot-light".

mc: copy dist/mc/*.ini to ~/.local/share/mc/skins/ and pick the skin in Options > Appearance, or run mc -S enot-dark-16M. The -16M variants are truecolor (mc >= 4.8.19 built with S-Lang, COLORTERM=truecolor); the 256 variants carry the xterm-256 depth of the spec, solved by the same max-min optimizer; on a terminal without truecolor mc does not degrade a skin per color but falls back to the default skin entirely, so the 256 variant is required there.

ranger: copy dist/ranger/colorschemes/enot.py to ~/.config/ranger/colorschemes/ and add set colorscheme enot to rc.conf. The scheme assigns roles via ANSI indices 0-15 and takes the actual colors from the terminal palette (wezterm/enot-*.toml), so a single file serves both themes; requires ranger >= 1.9.3.

Telegram Desktop: open dist/telegram/enot-dark.tdesktop-theme or dist/telegram/enot-light.tdesktop-theme via Settings > Chat settings > Choose from file, or send the file to any chat and click it - Telegram previews the theme before applying. The file is a theme package: a full palette (586 entries) rendered from the spec plus a solid bg0 background that replaces the wallpaper, so chats sit on a specification color; translucent entries (shadows, scrims, selection overlays) carry an alpha channel over specification colors.

## Palette values (sRGB hex; accents also as CIELAB LCh)

### dark theme

- bg0: #292826
- bg1: #323130
- bg2: #3e3d3c
- bg3: #4e4d4c
- fg0: #e2d7b5
- fg1: #beb69c
- comment: #83817e
- linenr: #5f5e5c
- red: #d7a0a1 (L* 71, C* 22, h 20)
- orange: #ad8764 (L* 59, C* 26, h 68)
- yellow: #fde494 (L* 91, C* 42, h 93)
- green: #bab04d (L* 71, C* 52, h 100)
- aqua: #9ff5e3 (L* 91, C* 30, h 179)
- blue: #79cad9 (L* 77, C* 26, h 216)
- purple: #c37fa1 (L* 61, C* 32, h 347)

### dark theme, ANSI 16

- black: #45423c
- red: #cb938b
- green: #b0a76c
- yellow: #a38e6a
- blue: #4eb3c7
- magenta: #d18da8
- cyan: #4caaa2
- white: #d7d1c6
- br_black: #6f6d6a
- br_red: #e0b1a5
- br_green: #c6c487
- br_yellow: #fcdfa5
- br_blue: #75e1f4
- br_magenta: #f0b7cd
- br_cyan: #7ce2dc
- br_white: #f8f3ec

### light theme

- bg0: #f4f2ee
- bg1: #e7e5e1
- bg2: #d7d6d2
- bg3: #c4c2be
- fg0: #48443e
- fg1: #64605c
- comment: #9b9893
- linenr: #b3b0ab
- red: #9d5a5d (L* 46, C* 30, h 20)
- orange: #632f05 (L* 26, C* 40, h 59)
- yellow: #876923 (L* 46, C* 42, h 84)
- green: #495a38 (L* 36, C* 22, h 127)
- aqua: #09797c (L* 46, C* 28, h 200)
- blue: #0c6a96 (L* 42, C* 32, h 255)
- purple: #974164 (L* 40, C* 40, h 356)

### light theme, ANSI 16

- black: #4a4641
- red: #a25756
- green: #5c5b37
- yellow: #836933
- blue: #0d7381
- magenta: #944c6b
- cyan: #2c7552
- white: #cac6bf
- br_black: #7b7670
- br_red: #632e28
- br_green: #50530f
- br_yellow: #493603
- br_blue: #06444d
- br_magenta: #5d1c3e
- br_cyan: #105958
- br_white: #eeeeee
